content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                                    Date : Feb. 3, 1977

                                                            cc Dawn
                                                            777-02-159

From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Ken Pinney

Subject: Telephone conversation with Justice Raymond Sullivan.

Justice Sullivan called today and asked a series of questions
related to the completion of his Statement of Economic Interests
(Form 721). The following represent the questions and my
responses:

1) He asked whether the interest on bonds issued by water
districts, school districts or the East Bay Municipal Utilities
District is income and, hence, reportable. I responded that
the interest was not income pursuant to Section 82030(b)(7) be-
cause the entities which he named are government agencies.
Accordingly, the interest is not reportable.

2) He asked whether his contributions to the judges' retire-
ment plan constituted a reportable investment. I responded
that it did not constitute a reportable investment because the
retirement plan was not a business entity within the meaning
of the Act. Section 82005.

3) He asked whether income received by his spouse which was her
separate property is reportable. I responded that such income
is not reportable since only income of his spouse in which he has
a community property interest creates a reporting obligation.
Section 82030(a).

4) He asked a series of questions about the reporting of gifts.
   a. He inquired whether he would have to report the amount
of a dinner which he received as a gift. The dinner was a
large dinner for approximately 60 distinguished members of
the Bar and apparently was paid for by 10 other members of
the Bar. He indicated that he could provide information
relative to the date on which the dinner took place and that
he assumed the dinner was worth more than $25, but he did not
know the precise value. I responded that under these circum-
stances it was not necessary to report the precise value.
He could insert a note into his Statement indicating that he
did not know the precise value but knew that it exceeded $25
and therefore was reporting the gift.

   b. He received a gift from 56 members of the Bar. The gift
was a watch and is valued in excess of $25. He wondered how
to report the donors. I indicated that when the number of

10

File
Feb. 3, 1977


donors is so large, it is permissible to describe them by
some appropriate appellation. In other words, it would be
sufficient if he described the donors as 56 past and present
members of the Bar.

c. He receives complimentary books
from publishing companies such as West's which are his per-
sonal property but are used in connection with his work.
I stated that it thought such books constituted informational
material within the meaning of Section 82028 and hence are
not reportable as a gift. He indicated that he might re-
port it anyway and I stated that to err on the side of
caution is, of course, permissible.

d. He and his wife received tickets to an event, and each
ticket was valued at $20. He wondered whether this created
any reporting obligation. I stated that the ticket received
by his wife was her separate property since gifts are sepa-
rate property and that, accordingly, no reporting obligation
ensued because the gift received by him was only for, $20.


RF:jo

sections.question_synthetic: Justice Raymond Sullivan sought clarification on multiple reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act regarding income from government bonds, retirement plan contributions, spouse's separate property income, and various gifts received by him and his spouse.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that interest from government agency bonds and retirement plan contributions are not reportable; spouse's separate property income requires no reporting; dinner gifts may be reported without precise value if unknown but noted as exceeding $25; large numbers of donors may be described by appropriate appellation; complimentary informational materials are not reportable as gifts; and gift tickets valued at $20 or less do not create reporting obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Internal FPPC memorandum documenting telephone conversation; no formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION headers but clear Q&A structure throughout; advice addresses multiple related topics with numbered sub-questions; initialed by 'KF:jo' indicating internal staff involvement

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Justice Raymond Sullivan sought clarification on multiple reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act regarding income from government bonds, retirement plan contributions, spouse's separate property income, and various gifts received by him and his spouse.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advised that interest from government agency bonds and retirement plan contributions are not reportable; spouse's separate property income requires no reporting; dinner gifts may be reported without precise value if unknown but noted as exceeding $25; large numbers of donors may be described by appropriate appellation; complimentary informational materials are not reportable as gifts; and gift tickets valued at $20 or less do not create reporting obligations.
embedding.first_500_words: This memorandum, dated February 3, 1977, is from the Fair Political Practices Commission to File. It documents a telephone conversation with Justice Raymond Sullivan regarding questions related to his Statement of Economic Interests (Form 721). Justice Sullivan asked several questions about reportable income and investments: 1) Whether interest on bonds issued by water districts, school districts, or the East Bay Municipal Utilities District is income and hence reportable. The response was that such interest is not reportable because the entities issuing the bonds are government agencies. 2) Whether contributions to the judges' retirement plan constitute a reportable investment. The response was that they do not, as the retirement plan is not a business entity within the meaning of the Act. 3) Whether income received by his spouse which is her separate property is reportable. The response was that such income is not reportable since only income of his spouse in which he has a community property interest creates a reporting obligation. 4) A series of questions about the reporting of gifts: a. Whether he would have to report the amount of a dinner received as a gift. The dinner was for approximately 60 distinguished members of the Bar and was paid for by 10 other members. He indicated he could provide information relative to the date but did not know the precise value. The response was that under these circumstances it was not necessary to report the precise value. He could insert a note indicating he did not know the precise value but knew it exceeded $25. b. He received a gift from 56 members of the Bar, valued in excess of $25. He wondered how to report the donors. The response was that when the number of donors is so large, it is permissible to describe them by some appropriate appellation. In other words, it would be sufficient if he described the donors as 56 past and present members of the Bar. c. He receives complimentary books from publishing companies such as West's which are his personal property but are used in connection with his work. I stated that I thought such books constituted informational material within the meaning of Section 82028 and hence not reportable as a gift. He indicated that he might report it anyway and I stated that to err on the side of caution is, of course, permissible. d. He and his wife received tickets to an event, and each ticket was valued at $20. He wondered whether this created any reporting obligation. I stated that the ticket received by his wife was her separate property since gifts are separate property and that, accordingly, no reporting obligation ensued because the gift received by him was only for $20. KF:jo
embedding.summary: A February 3, 1977 FPPC memorandum documenting telephone advice to Justice Raymond Sullivan regarding multiple questions about what constitutes reportable income, investments, and gifts under California's Political Reform Act, with specific guidance on Statement of Economic Interests (Form 721) filing requirements.
